focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

By noon the day after the wake, the F train spat Marisol out at Stillwell with a blast of platform heat that lasted exactly one staircase. At street level the wind took her jaw in both hands and twisted. She pulled her scarf up until wool scraped her mouth, then walked toward the boardwalk with her shoulders pitched forward like she was climbing, not crossing flat ground. January had stripped Coney down to steel, plywood, and bolts. Ride cars sat locked in place over the empty lots, each one with a skin of salt dust. The vendors' shutters were down. The gulls had the place to themselves and sounded meaner when there were no children throwing fries.

She had picked that hour because noon looked less dramatic in court. Noon was practical. Noon was two adults meeting in daylight to discuss publication boundaries, not a daughter arranging an ambush after a public funeral fight. She repeated that to herself as she stepped onto the boards and felt the hollowness under them, the old wood knocking lightly against its own nails when the gusts hit. Her phone kept buzzing inside her coat pocket. Unknown number, unknown number, then one text from a local arts reporter who had somehow found the prepaid line she had activated before dawn: Are you prepared to comment on allegations of defamation by the Rivas estate? She turned the phone facedown without opening the message.

Gloria stood half a block away near a locked lemonade stand, exactly where she had said she would be. She wore the same black puffer she wore to church and to funerals, too warm for buses, never warm enough for wind. Her hair was pinned so tight it made her cheekbones look sharper. One gloved hand held a dented blue thermos by the neck. The other rubbed her right knee in small circles through her woo...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...tes later came Malcolm Price, subject line in all caps: NOTICE OF PRESERVATION DEMAND. Keep all materials. Do not alter. Do not disseminate. Direct all correspondence through counsel.

Preserve. Do not alter. Do not disseminate. Malcolm's subject line sat in all caps while the radiator knocked twice and spat lukewarm air that smelled like dust.

Marisol opened the IR composite again and zoomed until the hidden shoulder broke into pixels. G.V., Dec 1991, cinco meses floated under black paint like chalk trapped in dirty water. She set that printout beside Salvador's unfinished execution page; meltwater from her jeans cuff wicked into the lower margin and curled it.

At midnight she clipped the release pages to Ada's chain-of-custody report hard enough to leave half-moons in the paper and wrote a sticky note in block letters: `HE DIED BEFORE THE WITNESS LINE. SHE SENT THE FILES ANYWAY.`

Then she opened a clean notebook page.

She wrote: `At 8:47 a.m. Salvador Rivas died before completing the execution page.`
She wrote: `At 5:58 p.m. Dr. Ada Park transmitted preservation records with hashes and metadata to legal, Nora Feldman, and me.`
She started a third line: `I have these records because she chose consequence over cover,` then struck through `chose consequence over cover` until the paper fuzzed and wrote above it, `refused to click acknowledge on an order to relabel the files.`

She capped the pen, uncapped it, and added one more line in smaller letters: `Do not spend her suspension on pretty sentences.`

Marisol set the blue day-one chip on top of the stack. The metal clicked once against the binder clip. She turned off the lamp and counted radiator knocks in the dark until her breathing slowed enough to sleep.
By dawn, the first calls about Saint Brigid's would begin.
